title: Network create test: nslookup: timed out, no servers could be reached
body:
Twice in the same Zuul run ([podman](https://fedora.softwarefactory-project.io/logs/51/51/555a5a504dd538d5be581f76a5a1fe72aec1e059/check/rpm-test/704d387/artifacts/test.podman-rootless-cgroupsv2.bats.log) and [podman-remote](https://fedora.softwarefactory-project.io/logs/51/51/555a5a504dd538d5be581f76a5a1fe72aec1e059/check/rpm-test/704d387/artifacts/test.podman-remote-rootless.bats.log), both rootless):
```console
not ok 249 podman network create
$ podman run -d --network testnet-MCsl9jiJ42 -p 127.0.0.1:54322:54322 quay.io/libpod/testimage:20210610 nc -l -n -v -p 54322
14bd66a379be97bb057d2fb5c509ccab621c6ca6d64557b104ec3b8235a63237
$ podman exec 14bd66a379be97bb057d2fb5c509ccab621c6ca6d64557b104ec3b8235a63237 nslookup google.com
;; connection timed out; no servers could be reached
[ rc=1 (** EXPECTED 0 **) ]
```
I have a vague memory that this happened on one of last week's builds, but went away when I pressed Rerun.

I can't reproduce on a 1minutetip VM. The string "`no servers could be reached`" does not appear in my flake logs. It only seems to happen in Zuul (it also happened [last week](https://fedora.softwarefactory-project.io/logs/50/50/ec6ea5bdc9b449e8d5c0029d72406a5adf253822/check/rpm-test/0031722/artifacts/)). I have no idea what the Zuul environment looks like, but it _has_ to have working network: podman couldn't pull images otherwise.

This isn't super-critical: we don't use Zuul except for fedpkg PRs. But if Zuul is seeing it, real users might be seeing it too. Reasons for filing:
* Can anyone see an obvious reason why this would fail under Zuul?
* If not, can we add debug info to the test to help diagnose?
* If not, can the test be rewritten so it uses something other than `nslookup`?

comments.body: Could change the tests to `ping -c 1 google.com`, but I am not sure if this would not fail also. Basically the tests is supposed to be checking if dns works, and it looks like dns is failing with no nodes available.

Hmmmmmm..... I was right in the middle of responding when I had an idea: is it possible that `nslookup` uses `/etc/resolv.conf`, but the host itself uses the new systemd-type name resolution? I remember having nightmareish problems when my Fedora "upgraded" to use that, and me having to disable it, but don't remember what those problems were. If that's the problem, then `ping` might actually work because `google.com` would be resolved by the system, not `nslookup`. But then again I really have no clue.

Yeah I would prefer to keep nslookup because this only uses the dns protocol.

Assuming the system has a working internet connection and a correctly configured `/etc/resolv.conf` on the host then one following problems could happen:
1. The container has the wrong nameserver ip in `/etc/resolv.conf`
2. The dnsmasq process for this network is misconfigured or died.
3. The rootless CNI slirp4netns process died.
4. The `/etc/resolv.conf` in the rootless CNI namespace was setup incorrectly.
Should the test cat out the /etc/resolv.conf when it fails. Or always so we can at least see it.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

Yesterday's [3.4.4-1.fc36 build](https://bodhi.fedoraproject.org/updates/FEDORA-2021-dd9a3bc3d2) passed its gating tests; the [previou build](https://bodhi.fedoraproject.org/updates/FEDORA-2021-675fab67a0) failed, but not with this error. Closing until it happens again.
